MA	S	T	E	R	MI	N	D
G	R	OU	P	S	:
S	AL	E	SAND OP	E	R	AT	I	ONS
F	r	o	m T	h	eG	K	I	C	/
D	a	nK	e	n	n	e	d	yA	r	c	h	i	v	e	s
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 20 --

REPRINTED FROM
DAN S. KENNEDY’S No B.S. Information Marketing Letter
APRIL, 2013, 2012
Each Special Report brings you an extended length dissertation on one aspect of info-
marketing. These Reports alternate every other month with Issues of The No B.S.
INFO-Marketing Letter*; 6 times a year, a new Report, 6 times a year, the newsletter.
The Letter/Reports are included in Information Marketing Association Membership as a
Member Benefit (www.info-marketing.org).
*NOT to be confused with the regular No B.S. Marketing Letter or The No B.S. Marketing To The Affluent
Letter also published by GKIC.)
© 2014/GKIC
*********************************************************************************
The Information Marketing Association provides Insider Secrets, Access to Experts and Step-by-Step
Resources Assembled for Creating and Running Simple and Easy Multimillion-Dollar Information
Marketing Businesses
Successful authors, self-publishers of books, people who sell home study courses, personal and
business coaches, newsletter publishers, and seminar promoters turn to the Information Marketing
Association for the critical insights on the business side of running a successful information marketing
business. This information gap can only be filled by the one and only trade association for the
information marketing industry.
The IMA offers everything you need to continue to grow your information marketing business under
one umbrella. The benefits are too many to list.
Because you have this report, you can experience all of the benefits for free, for 2-months, with an
invitation only opportunity to get a two month trial membership. Just visit www.IMAFree.com to take
advantage of this opportunity, while it lasts.
*********************************************************************************
1
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 20 --

Before we get to this month’s topic – the mastermind group – I want to say a few things about my
own Titanium Mastermind Group for info-marketers, which I run for GKIC. We had our first meeting
of the year in March, with 100% attendance, and it was electric. Outstanding. The caliber of
individuals who have come together in this group is really quite incredible, and I thought you might
find it interesting, so here goes:
Steve Adams – first time in a group with me, referred by Craig Simpson. Steve has a chain of pet
stores powered by my kind of marketing, brilliantly executed. He is opening new stores and
acquiring and converting others. Within existent stores, double digit percentage growth each year
for the past three years. He is now layering an info-marketing business onto it, with a range of info
products and membership for the 100,000+ customers that patronize his stores. We believe he’ll
have a multi-million dollar info biz within its first year.
Mike Crow – his niche info business is with the home inspection industry, and in a small, largely
blue collar niche, he has developed a thriving, lucrative “classic model” info-business, with entry
level products, membership/continuity, ascension, a major annual conference, coaching and done-
for-them services. His latest conference – incorporating brainstorming from last year’s Titanium
meetings – were dramatically higher in revenue and other targeted results than prior year. Mike
also has a “Coaching Blueprint” business, providing turn-key content and support for info-
marketers starting or operating coaching businesses. (Renewed from Titanium 2012)
Curtis Dawn – a very successful marketer of income-from-home and home-based business
opportunities, in a transitional time. (Renewed from Titanium 2012)
Ben Glass – well-known to GKIC Members. His company, Great Legal Marketing, is the dominant,
complete info-marketing business serving the legal profession. Ben also operates his original
business, a thriving personal injury law practice.
Bill Gough – top Allstate agency owner turned info-marketer to Allstate agency owners and
agents. A very successful “classic” info-biz in a relatively small niche. This year, beginning the
process of figuring out a new life beyond the niche, in the insurance industry at large. Bill is also a
Private Client of mine. (Renewed from Titanium 2012)
Hudson – first time in a group with me, but a long, long-time Kennedy and GKIC member. Adams
provides a portfolio of done for them marketing services to the home services industries i.e. air
conditioning/heating contractors, plumbers, electricians, etc. He won my Ultimate Sales Letter
Contest some years back and declined the top prize of a Mustang in favor of a comprehensive
copy critique and phone consultation.
Chauncey Hutter Jr. – a Kennedy Lifer. Chauncey was in my previous Platinum mastermind
groups for a number of years. Famously first found me by buying a book of mine in a close-out bin
at an office supply store. For many years, his primary info marketing businesses have served the
accounting and tax preparation industry. Currently, he also has an info-business aimed at Christian
business owners and entrepreneurs.
2
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 20 --

Andrei Kossyrine – first time in a group with me, traveling 24 hours from Russia to be at these
meetings! An accomplished info-marketer, with emphasis on events, drawing thousands to physical
events and tens of thousands to webcast events.
Donna Krech – based on a unique, extremely successful brick-and-mortar health club/fitness
center, she has a franchise program, a range of info-products, coaching and well-attended events.
She is also working to broaden her focus, with programs for all kinds of entrepreneurs and
business owners. (Renewed from Titanium 2012)
Keith Lee – a lot of GKIC Members will know him, attached to 3DMailResults.com, a supplier to
info-marketers as well as myriad businesses downstream through info-marketers. His first
business, American Retail Supply, is a major supplier of everything from store fixtures to
promotional goods to retailers. His newest venture is info-marketing, beginning with a program for
management of staff and business operations and marketing.
Ron LeGrand – the king and elder statesman of the independent real estate investor market, Ron
has probably logged more miles, put more people through seminars, and actually made more
millionaires through real estate than any living human. Right now, you can hear his aggressive
radio advertising campaign on satellite and AM radio stations. Ron has been with me forever.
(Renewed from Titanium 2012)
Forrest Walden and Jim Cavale, Iron Tribe Fitness, and Andy Mackenser and Sean Kelly,
Healthy Vending. Franchisors in the health/fitness field, separate companies but also engaged in
joint ventures. They are marketing business programs requiring investments of $250,000.00 and
$60,000.00 respectively. They are also Private Clients. (Forrest and Jim are renewal upgrades
from Platinum 2012.)
Lily Noon – extremely successful, fascinating entrepreneur with several businesses. The pure
info-marketing one is as exclusive U.S. publisher, event promoter and representative for the most
feng shui expert in the world. There are seminars, coaching programs, certification programs, and
more. She has developed a marketing platform she now intends to add other complementary gurus
to. (Renewed from Titanium 2012)
Dr. Tom Orent – leader in dentistry; speaker, author, newsletter publisher with a very strong, high-
end coaching program. He has been with me for many years, and like Ron and Chauncey, was in
my original info-marketer mastermind groups for consecutive years.
Robin Robins – the only B2B info-marketer in the group, Robin has a complete info-biz serving
the IT industry, and independent IT consultants. Her retention success in her coaching programs is
exceptional, thanks in part to a proprietary “membership dashboard” with extremely high pain of
disconnect. This is her first time in a group with me.
Nelson Searcy – a pastor of several thriving churches in different U.S. cities and an international
ministry, Nelson is also an info-marketer to pastors worldwide, with a wide range of products and
publications and a robust coaching program, involving thousands of pastors. (Renewed from
Titanium 2012)
3
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 20 --

Craig Simpson – my “go to guy” for direct-mail, and many of my clients’ go-to guy as well. Craig is
a freelance direct mail project manager, and an absolute wizard at list analysis, selection, and
procurement from commercial sources. Craig also publishes a newsletter, has an info-product all
about direct-mail, and a coaching program as well. This is Craig’s first time in a group with me.
Richard Strauch – consultant and “done for them services” leader in the legal profession, with a
small numbers/high fee approach to coaching. This is his first time in a group with me.
Lou Vukas – a direct marketer of a number of different opportunity and investment information
products. This is his first time in a group with me.
Note: I have omitted listing spouses, partners, accompanying staff and others integrally involved in the operation of
these businesses and who attend and participate in the meetings.
Combined, I would estimate this group’s annual revenues from information marketing to exceed
$150-million and approach $300-million. As you can see, there is impressive diversity and level of
accomplishment in the group.
It is early in this year and there is no way to know if there will be openings, and if so, how many,
but if you believe you belong in this group and wish to place your name on a priority wait list, you
can do so directly with me, via fax to 602-269-3113. You should be an established info-marketer
with at least $1-million in info-marketing revenues or have exceptional success in a related
business and be in the process of integrating or adding info-marketing to it or launching an info-
marketing enterprise in addition to it. Those in the former category get priority over the latter.
4
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 20 --

Two topics – selling mastermind groups and operating mastermind
groups.
OPERATIONS
Over the years, I’ve run mastermind groups for info-marketers, for mixed breed of business
owners; butchers, bakers ‘n candlestick makers, and niche groups, such as chiropractors and
financial advisors. My longest tenure is with groups for info-marketers: my original Platinum Group
ran for 11 consecutive years and, after brief hiatus, I’m in my 2 nd year with the Titanium Group for
GKIC. Mine have always been year or two year terms, not open ended continuity, and I’ve always
had a very high year to year renewal rate. I believe I do a damn good job running these kinds of
groups and the meetings, if I do say so myself – although I’m sure if you ask past and current
members they will concur.
There are basically two structures of the groups and meetings. There is what I consider the true
mastermind meeting, with participants seated boardroom or U-shape style, and with every member
taking a turn during the course of the meeting. The other structure is what I affectionately call fake
mastermind. These groups are much larger, and are more workshops with hot seats for some
members, than they are mastermind meetings. The latter can be more lucrative and can certainly
benefit its participants, but my view is, it is not an application of the mastermind concept as
originally described by Napoleon Hill. That does not mean it isn’t valuable. It’s just not a real
mastermind group.
THE SMALL GROUP: LOGISTICS
The mastermind group meeting for 2 days maxxed out, in my experience, at 20 members. If you go
beyond that number, I can’t imagine how you give each member a fair turn, a reasonable amount
of time and provide enough value yourself through your contributions. This year, I have 19 in
Titanium, and we need one full day (8 AM to 5:30 PM) plus a short evening (6:30 PM to 8:30 or
9:00 PM) and a 2 nd day ending at 4:00 PM. This taxes their ability to focus.
I choose to stay in the front of the room on a stool; I currently have each person come up for their
turns; at other times I’ve had them stay in their seats. It is my belief the leader needs to lead from
the front and in an authority position, although there are differences of opinion about this. I was
actually surprised to discover when auditing the last meetings of GKIC’s Platinum and Titanium
groups when Bill Glazer was running them that he stayed in a seat at the U-table right along with
the other members. I prefer a good-sized rather than cramped room, although I’ve worked in and
groups can work in either.
My basic format for each person’s turn is approximately 25% of their time sharing contacts,
resources and recommendations they judge helpful to all, 25% “what is” with their business,
including report on items discussed in prior meeting now acted on, 25% presentation of
problem/opportunity, initiative, strategy, etc. and several related, specific questions they want to
discuss and get my input and group input on, 25% that discussion. You certainly don’t have to use
my format, but you need a format that participants understand and reasonably adhere to. With that
said, different people have different needs/interests and reasons for being in a group that need to
be flexibly accommodated. For example, some are there seeking a lot of accountability, thus the
reporting is important to them, while others want no accountability, so the reporting on
implementation gets short shrift. Some are there for approval, encouragement and recognition, and
5
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 20 --

showing off successes and knowledge is most important to them. Others are there, nose to
grindstone, blinders on, seeking advice. The value the individual wants has to be juggled with value
to the group from every person’s time.
To my mind the best virtue of the small, true mastermind group is the high caliber and close
commonality you can create in the participants. This assures that there will be a lot of value for
everybody throughout the meeting, not just in their turn. Thus, from an operational standpoint, not
just a sales standpoint, the small number of available spots leveraged against a large list of
candidates are the best position to be in.
THE BIG GROUP: LOGISTICS
The bigger groups can accommodate a larger to a near infinite number i.e. all you can sell number
of members, because they are not true masterminds. Structurally, the meetings tend to include (1)
lecture (by leader and/or guest expert(s) and/or participant(s) with special expertise); (2) workshop
style activities – egs. seated 8 at round tables, each table has a discussion and/or activity, then a
table spokesperson reports; and (3) some version of “hot seats” when selected members get a turn
to discuss their specific matters. As with the small group, different people have different
needs/interests and reasons for being in a group that need to be flexibly accommodated. Usually,
the caliber of the group is not as high and uniform of quality (however defined) than the small
group, for the obvious reason that you are eager to sell more into the bigger group, and are
therefore less selective. The bigger groups usually have poorer year to year or long term retention,
typically no more than 2 to 3 years per participant, and the 1 year and out rate is high, so you need
a source and means of re-filling the larger group continuously. (Note: small groups are often re-
stocked annually or episodically, but big groups tend to need continues feed.)
CONTRIBUTIONS BY THE LEADER
I have seen successful groups with vastly different levels of leader contribution – ranging from a
simple facilitator, who barely contributes input but basically referees and lets the group do all the
work to my extreme, where I am very much involved, providing the bulk of the input and advice to
each participant, and often calling on members with precisely relevant experience to comment. At
the first extreme, the groups are sometimes called ‘peer advisory groups’ and are essentially
absent a coach and coaching element. Arguably, these are the purest form of the mastermind
alliance concept. At my extreme, a large part of the value members get and reason they are in the
group is the leader’s expertise and advisor/coach role, and it is essentially mastermind+coaching. I
think it is easier to have authority and maintain control with my approach, but it is also a lot more
work and more stressful and fatiguing, and also not multipliable, with others running groups you fill
as rainmaker.
It’s important to have clarity yourself about what role(s) you or your appointed group leader will fill,
and to manage the group’s expectations about that from the outset.
Personally, I like to bring a bound manual or stack of stuff to each meeting, which is either
unique/exclusive to the group or that they are getting ahead of the entire GKIC membership. Time
permitting, I also like bringing at least one topic, one set of information that I contribute, drawn from
my own activities or (with their permission) a client’s. Some leaders just show up and lead, and if
they can get away with it, more power to ‘em.
6
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 20 --

In many groups, especially the large groups, there is a strong desire for something “NEW” at every
meeting. The facts that they have all sorts of strategies and tools that are working, and those that
they might be better served focusing on better and more complete implementation of those is
irrelevant; they want new.
THE PORTAL + COACHING/MASTERMIND GROUP
At A-to-Z Blueprints in April, I discussed a relatively new format that I ran (with my client, Matt
Zagula) with a group of financial advisors, 2011-2012, and hope to run in another industry
beginning later this year. The chief purpose of the group is to have exclusive marketing tools,
programs and content built for it and vendors introduced, to implement the programs. The coaching
and/or mastermind aspects are secondary. In a way, the group is a “co-op” getting expertise and
services no one member could afford alone. This means that the leader will be bringing two to four
new turn-key programs to each meeting, but most of the work of presenting and explaining (and
selling) those programs will fall on the expert vendors as guest presenters. This has a lot of Tom
Sawyer/Paint The Fence i.e. free labor built into it. (It can also lend itself to corporate sponsorship.)
DISCIPLINE
In a small group, there will typically be very strong, successful players who are not used to
sacrificing doing as they please in favor of a collective exercise. They are hard to herd. In a big
group, there is simply that; a big group, and just getting them into their seats on time and engaged
is troublesome. There’s a reason school teachers whine about class size. Either way, it is my
opinion the leader has responsibility to all to keep the trains running on time, to fairly apportion time
yet allow some flexibility depending on interest and value of any given discussion to all, and avoid
letting the thing slip out of his control. These groups can have a bully, they can have a constant
complainer, they can have an overly argumentative or critical know it all, they can have an overly
sensitive child. Mostly, private, one to one corrective conversations should occur – taking an
offender aside and politely but firmly identifying the behavior and asking for change. Occasionally,
though, correction visible to the group is required. I have separated multi-millionaires who couldn’t
stop whispering to each other and re-arranged their seating. I have given time-outs. Confiscated
phones. And so on.
Egregiously bad behavior must bear consequences. If one is allowed to get away with something,
six’ll be doing it next meeting. With small groups with exceptionally busy people, leaving early the
2 nd day is always a problem. Invariably, the person has good reason, and is only cheating the end
a little – but this is unfair to those there. My approach to this is to place that person last at the next
meeting, and caution them that repeat offense means expulsion. I use a volunteer system for who
goes when, although I may shuffle the volunteers a little for reasons of my own. Meeting to
meeting, those who went late the 2 nd day have right to go first half of first day; those who went early
first day are pushed to second day.
CHEATING
There are two kinds of cheating. The most egregious is taking and not sharing or giving. That
person must be sternly counseled and if he repeats his one-way behavior, ejected. You can
choose to let this slide in a big group because it can be hidden to a great extent – for example, a
person can go all year without ever taking a hot seat. Sharing “what works” can be voluntary, with
some sitting it out. But in a small group, it is blatantly obvious, unjust, and cannot be permitted. The
true mastermind meeting is no place for the person obsessed with confidentiality and protection of
7
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 20 --

all his information but eager to breech everyone else’s. The other kind of cheating is nearly as
selfish: non-attentiveness. The person who wants everyone tuned in to his turn, but is mucking
about on his laptop, doing other work, napping, or out of the room during others’ turns must be
called on it and must fully participate or be ejected.
FAIR USE
In many groups as in mine, members will be doing Show ‘N Tell, sharing ads, marketing
campaigns, or other material that is working for them, that they have invested in developing, and
that they hold copyright to. This is very tricky territory. Generally, I think the same fair use rule,
fuzzy as it may be, applies in this environment as it does when examples are shown off in other
environment. This means that examples are for inspiration, not verbatim or near verbatim theft. If
someone is in a completely different niche than another and liberally moves what is shown, that is
of less consequence than if the two are in the same niche. If a member wants to make complete
use of something, he should discuss licensing it from its creator/owner. And no should mean no.
CONFIDENTIALITY
In small groups, there is typically a high level of disclosure, and people must be able to trust the
others not to gossip, rumor-monger, openly discuss in social media, or otherwise violate obvious
confidences in unguarded conversation. If in doubt, they should ask. If this trust does not exist,
members will hold back information and ideas, and soon the group will consist of all takers, no
givers. In a larger group, there is naturally, automatically less disclosure. In every case, it must be
made clear that you, the group operator, owner and leader, accept no responsibility for protecting
anyone’s confidential matters. I usually permit individuals to record their turn at bat for their
purposes, but no one can record the entire meeting. (I do record for archival purposes and store for
years, in event of any legal issues.)
DEMONSTRATING & DOCUMENTING VALUE
Many people have and report incredible breakthroughs and gains by being in these groups. You
want to capture these in writing, so that you are able to “re-motivate” a member with poor memory,
who is becoming disenchanted and sour, or considering leaving or not renewing. In addition, as a
leader, when value occurs in the room, it’s important to call attention to it. If an action that will
create income or other material gain has been developed and agreed to, it’s good to calculate the
worth to the member in front of everybody. If you bundle goods and services, members need
reminded of what they are saving by not buying those same goods and services separately. I have
a couple clients who send out zero balance, itemized invoices each month with their packages, and
I think it’s smart.
ACCOUNTABILITY
Not everybody responds well to it, even some who insist they want it, so imposing formal
accountability on all has to be carefully considered. On the other hand, some people belong to a
group because it forces them to get things done with next meeting or coaching call as their
deadline. I favor voluntary accountability and recognition.
MAKING MONEY IN ADDITION TO PARTICIPATION FEES
Sponsors: more so with large groups than small, but sometimes even with small, there is
opportunity to get costs paid by sponsors, or even costs plus promotional fees from sponsors. In
one recent mastermind/coaching situation, my client had 100% of the actual meeting expenses –
including meeting space, food and beverages, and a paid guest speaker – paid for by a corporate
8
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 20 --

sponsor, for the entire 2 years of the group’s existence. There is no reason not to think this
through.
Commissions: obviously, expert vendors you create opportunity for via your groups owe you tolls.
The privilege should never be abused; a mastermind/coaching meeting should never become a
“sell-a-thon”.
Consulting, Etc.: your mastermind members will invariably take initiative in asking you for additional
one to one consulting/coaching or for other professional services you may provide. In my case,
consulting engagements, copywriting engagements, speaking engagements, co-authors (for fees)
have all arisen from the mastermind groups I’ve conducted. I do not discount, as I have more
demand than supply. Years back, when that was not the case, I did grant fee discounts to the
group members as an added value benefit.
“The Taste”: for the first handful of years of my original Platinum Group, I took a 1% “royalty” from
each side of any deal created between members as a result of ideas or discussions occurring in
the meetings (thus 2% total) – a strategy given to me by Henry Kissinger. While I made a 6-figure
sum in total, I later abandoned it and raised fees instead, avoiding the obvious problem of
determining which things the % applied to and which it didn’t. However, in the right circumstances,
I wouldn’t hesitate to do it again.
Percentage Above Base: there are coaches who layer P.O.B. on top of a fee. A base is established
– typically the participant’s income + year to year growth he’s achieved on his own, average of last
3 years + inflation, then the coach gets an agreed upon percentage of all new income above the
base. This is much too complex a subject for proper discussion here, but I mention it for sake of
thoroughness.
Grooming Successors: the high-end mastermind groups you run can be excellent incubators, for
coaches you subsequently integrate into your business, or even for buyers of your business. (Bill
Glazer was a consulting client, then a member of my original Platinum Group before I sold my
business to him.)
LEGAL MATTERS
I’m not a lawyer so I’m not dispensing legal advice. Should legal advice seem appropriate, please
secure it from a professional. I will mention that you can face attempts to hold you liable for your
advice, for costs people incur in following it, for business conducted between group members you
introduce and appear to endorse, for alienation of spousal affection, and so on. I have personally
experienced only one such problem in my entire career, but I have seen it all. There is specific
consultant liability insurance available, as well as the option of inflating the cap on your general
umbrella policy. There is also your contract and/or post-contract “rules of participation”
correspondence with members, as well as recorded verbal statements, where you can place
appropriate disclaimers and secure liability waivers.
BETWEEN MEETINGS
Most mastermind programs have actual coaching elements, so there are group and/or one on one
calls in the months between the meetings. If this is not the case or in addition to those calls, some
month to month, if not week to week contact is desirable for two reasons – one, you don’t want the
value assessment to become fee divided by three meetings equals cost per meeting. In the GKIC
system, mastermind/coaching group members receive Diamond Membership bundled in, so they
9
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 20 --

are hearing from me weekly via my personal fax, and three times monthly via the two newsletter
packages and a tele-seminar. Titanium Members also receive this newsletter.
“BUNDLES” & PERKS
There is temptation to bundle too much in, and a worse temptation to make paid items (like events)
free to mastermind members. You have to honestly and carefully evaluate the economics of
this: the gross revenue from any items they would likely buy if not in mastermind gets
deducted from the net of the mastermind fee (after all expenses), to reveal what you are
really paid for mastermind. On the other hand, you need mastermind members participating in
most or all of the other goods and services you offer. In some cases, you can make other
purchasing pre-requisite or mandatory to be permitted into the mastermind. I am particularly
opposed to letting mastermind members attend free-standing events free, and prefer deep
discounts but still their skin in the game. The perk members typically want most is access to the
leader, and that must be carefully controlled – but never “loose” so it can be taken for granted.
MAJOR MISTAKES
1: In my opinion, being a buddy, hanging out at the bar, and otherwise socializing with mastermind
members is a mistake. Familiarity breeds contempt. It diminishes your impact in the meetings. It
runs risk of you doing something embarrassing that severely damages your authority. It should go
without saying that sexual liaisons with members are unacceptable. A “partying culture” is not
conducive to real mastermind alliance. Having said that, it is MY opinion and modus operandi.
There are mastermind groups run by leaders who party hard with the group, and even have the
social aspect as a big part of the lure of the group. I am not convinced this is sustainable. After all, I
don’t pay my friends to hang out with them.
2: If your group has a term, waiting too late in the term to secure renewals and failing to build
interest, ideally a waiting list for openings, will get you in trouble.
3: Padding the room. There ARE a few “gurus” who claim to be running high fee mastermind
groups, but 2/3rds of the group are comp’d or one meeting invited guests. It’s a sham, and it’s hard
to hold it together.
BIGGEST STRUCTURAL DECISION
Term or Drop-In. Larger groups can more easily be designed to be on-going, and to have new
members drop in at any time, which lends itself to marketing immediately preceding every meeting,
with the impending event of the upcoming meeting providing urgency. Bad news: if they can drop in
any time, they can drop out any time – and they do. Term gives you a single sales cycle each year
(although promotion is continuous) and a renewal problem, but it restricts drop-outs to near zero,
and supports exclusivity and urgency when offered. There is also the issue of Alumni groups or
programs for those completing a term program – which typically involves price reduction.
SALES
ASCENSION
The best mastermind groups tend to be filled by ascension, so that your good customers who are
conversant with what you teach and are succeeding by using it comprises the group. This typically
means that you own built-in opportunities to fill and re-stock your group, within your own media and
events. In that kind of environment, it is important to shine the spotlight on your mastermind
10
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 20 --

members as often and as much as you can (without sacrificing delivery of value to all readers,
listeners or attendees), so that getting into the group is seen as a desirable aspiration. Not a week
goes by that I don’t get a letter or two from members that includes them expressing the ambition of
being able to qualify for and get into a mastermind group with me in the future. Whether those
individuals do or not, it evidences that I am getting it into many minds. A true ascension model is
ladder-like and has pre-requisites, so that somebody moves from level to level to level and can’t
skip any. Some info-businesses have this, as I did in the early years. Others permit any member of
their largest list to “leapfrog” up and into their top level(s), given certain qualifications – or, for
some, have pulse and wallet, they’re in.
The most important thing to know, I think, is that if a group is filled with difficulty, it will be in
constant jeopardy, and the leader will be weakened; if a group is filled easily, and that is visible and
known, the group will be more stable and the leader strengthened. This is almost entirely a
function of numbers: total, active customers to draw from; flow of new customer’s month by
month. Typically, a lot of new blood is needed. The reasons for that are complex and I’m not going
to take a detour into them here. A good big-thumb number is 1% = easy, 5% = difficult, beyond 5%
very, very difficult if not impossible. By that I mean, if the number of positions to be filled in a
mastermind group = 1% of your active customer base, it should be easy to fill. If = 5%, it’s difficult
but doable. If it will take more than 5% of your active customer base to fill and maintain the group,
you’re in trouble, especially 2 nd year, 3 rd year, etc.
Big groups are, in many ways, harder to fill than small. They obviously have more seats to sell, but
beyond that, they tend to be less appealing thing to sell. The #1 offset is usually price; these
groups usually sell for 50% less than a higher level small group in the same ascension ladder or
pyramid. One frequently used trick is to make the group a natural, seamless extension of a
precedent product or event, and some info-marketers go so far as to make the monthly payment
identical – this is expressed like this: “The System is $397 a month for 2 months, during which you
get Mastermind Coaching free. Beginning the 3 rd month you continue in the Mastermind Group at
$397 a month.” That strategy is best used with sales surges, so that everybody buying, let’s say in
September, is funneled into their first Mastermind Meeting either in October before the
continuation, or in November the first month of continuation.
It is very hard to make ascension work without very visible status for those ascended into the group
or groups, within the customer community or, ideally, also to the outside world. Info-marketers who
focus only on the value proposition and omit this reveal a lack of understanding of what drives
buying behavior in general, and more so within a closed community of any kind.
DIRECT – “COLD”
Quite a lot of coaching programs and mastermind groups are sold with no ascension involved,
direct to cafeteria customers or even to cold leads, typically with niche or subculture commonality. I
have created marketing for this, for more than two dozen clients. When you by-pass pyramid or
ladder and make this the front-end offer, you have to be prepared for a significant-sized cost per
sale. In niches, in instances I’ve worked with over the past 5 years, the CPS has ranged from
$6,000 to $16,000. (In an ascension model, the CPS can be nearly zero.) Often, this is the best
model for a leader very busy with a successful core business, for whom creating and running a
pyramid info-biz, with entry level products, continuity, events and ascension is impractical. It is also
ideal for the person who wants to lock in place a set amount of income or extra income outside his
core business and has no open-ended growth ambition.
11
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 20 --

APPLICATION MODEL
I first used the application model in the late 1970’s, when a detailed application process was
required of doctors before they were permitted into a 2 day meeting at which 3-year term coaching
(called: practice management) was sold. Joe Polish was one of the first of the info-marketers many
of you would know that I had use it; in his case, the application was prerequisite for invitation to a
closed teleconference, where the opportunity to join a group was presented. My Titanium Member
Dr. Tom Orent has relied on Application+Diagnostic Call for at least 10 years, maybe longer, in
successfully filling his high-end coaching programs, and has mastered it.
The power of this is in its psychology. First, few people apply for things they don’t intend to do or
buy if accepted. Application to college, loan application, etc. So the act of completing and
submitting an application creates impetus to buy when the good news arrives that they’ve qualified
and been accepted. Second, time invested equals commitment. (This applies to much more
than the Application Model, and most marketers and info-marketers do NOT grasp the importance
of this fact.) The more time a person invests, in this case in the application, the more invested he is
in successful outcome – this is joining. Walking away empty-handed after putting in time and going
to the trouble of applying feels like failure. It takes a doctor at least an hour to complete Dr. Orent’s
application, and he needs to fetch information from his files and financials to do it. Every once in a
while, an info-marketer even charges an application fee, which further strengthens commitment.
Third, the application can (and should) be structured to uncover, highlight and reinforce the
individual’s problems, frustrations, inadequacies and business deficiencies, so that his sense of
need to be in the program heightens as he completes the application. You have to be clever, not
clumsy about this. You highlight authentic need.
Looping back, this model works best when you need only 1% of the customer base to fill the group.
TELE-SEMINAR/WEBINAR
These are used three ways: one, as precursor to a sales process and presentation; two, as the
sales presentation; three, as follow-up to unconverted leads from a sales presentation. They can
be the step behind application, as mentioned above. In that case or otherwise, there can be pre-
disclosure, meaning people attending know they are coming to learn about and decide whether or
not they want to join the group or program. Or the program to be offered and fact of an offer
concealed, with attendees coming for teaching content, celebrity interview or other purpose, then
the offer is sprung on them.
I had a client for several years who would take the punishment of a marathon open Q&A call and
handle skeptics and cynics, which he did well. After every promotion of a mastermind group, if he
had capacity left, he would invite all the unconverted leads to a group call with a start time and no
end time, to answer questions and talk with as many who wanted to talk, for as long as it took, and
let everybody hear him work without a net. He sometimes had 100 to 300 at the start of the call, 10
or 30 at its end, and would sell his remaining positions.
DIAGNOSTIC CALL
By various means, the prospect is driven to a diagnostic or consultative call, a “sample” coaching
call, etc., in which he is engaged one to one with a “coach” (tele-shark) or in some cases with the
group leader himself. This can be done within an ascension model or from cold leads. Some info-
marketers pluck highly motivated leads out of their main marketing funnel, taking them out of it
12
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 20 --

through a side door into this kind of a call. (Sean Greeley has had this in his process). Others tie it
to product purchase, making the call a bonus gift or a forced part of a product purchase. This is
obviously manual labor intensive, and requires skilled labor, but it can be extremely effective. For a
number of info-marketers, this is THE model they use for their front-end sale and again for selling
their coaching, mastermind groups or done-for-them-services.
AT AN EVENT
Most info-marketers sell into coaching programs and mastermind groups at their own events,
including the equivalent of their annual or bi-annual conventions. It offers best opportunity, because
current mastermind members can be showcased and can testify; there is a super-charged
emotional atmosphere; red room-blue room manipulation is easy; event fees can be credit to
mastermind fees, etc. There are also events built entirely for the single purpose of selling the
mastermind event. Other events can feed into a group – at GKIC, a current approach is to hold
Fast Start Training for “young” members and the Peak Performers/Implementation Group’s
meeting on overlapping days in the same hotel, so the groups merge for meal/social functions, the
leaders can flip rooms and do double duty, and Peak/IC can be presented to Fast Start attendees
as a natural extension of the experience they’re having. This is another strategy that dates in my
experience back to the 70’s. There is also a model: massive online product launch sells product
with event ticket attached; the event is structured to sell coaching and/or mastermind group(s).
CAUTION: if you are entirely dependent on an annual event or periodic events to re-stock
mastermind groups or coaching groups, you live on thin ice.
DISCOVERY DAY
Typically used when skipping the pyramid and selling the group as the front-end offer to cold
prospects, but it can be used to draw from within a customer base as well. There is pre-disclosure;
attendees know they are coming to make a decision about joining a program, and they know a
great deal about it, including fee or fee range, in advance. Often, they also know to plan to stay for
a Fast Start Day if they join the group or program. I have helped a number of clients sell out
mastermind groups, larger coaching groups, portal groups and even franchises with this approach.
SAMPLING
While I am not a big fan of sampling (based on the prostitute reality – anticipation is often more
appealing than reality), I have put people into mastermind groups this way myself, occasionally,
and some info-marketers do it routinely. A prospect or small number of prospects are permitted to
“audit” an actual mastermind meeting in whole or first day only, as observers only (not
participants), then they join or not based on the actual experience. The trickiest part of this is not
de-valuing what the group members are paying for. Its virtue is efficiency. (It is particularly useful
with potential members referred or “nominated by” existent members.)
OPEN ENROLLMENT / SALES-Y SELLING
This usually applies to bigger groups. No illusion of exclusivity is attempted, and none exists. It is
sold to any and all, at a point in a sales funnel, at an event, or be periodic promotion to an entire
list.
FEATURES & BENEFITS
1: Antidote to Aloneness
2: Access to (High Level) Information
3: Power of Combined Experience
13
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 20 --

4: Efficiency
5: Speed from Combined Experimentation
6: Practical: Mutual Sharing of Resources; Alliances & JV’s
7: Extension Solutions & Opportunities
8: (Intimate / High-Level) Access to the Leader / Exclusivity
9: Status
10: Bundled Products, Services, Perks
11: Exclusive Products, Services (Portal)
12: Unique or Appealing Experience
1: The entrepreneur feels a lot of isolation. The mastermind group provides an environment with
friendly competition, mutual understanding, appreciation for his achievements i.e. a place he can
brag and get applause, appreciation for his problems i.e. a place he can get legitimate sympathy
and encouragement.
2 & 3: The combined past and current experience of 10, 12, 20 mastermind members with similar
yet not identical business or occupations, at similar levels of success – varying within a range, etc.
provides a much broader portfolio of information than can be gained by casual peer acquaintances
and conversations or study in isolation. The leader can also bring “advanced”, exclusive or
confidential information from his experience and sources and/or bring in experts one would rarely
get small group access to.
4: How else can you look under the hood – in detail - and learn and borrow from 10, 12 or 20
relevant businesses, in one place, with one trip, in two days?
5: When individuals in a group run different tests and experiments, reporting to each other,
everybody buys speeds. If, in a year, 20 members each run 3 different, significant experiments,
each has benefit of 60. At 3 per year himself, he’d need 20 years to match. (From an operational
standpoint, this can be informal and naturally occurring or orchestrated or both.)
6: Members can swap services with each other, endorse each others’ books, can create products
or promotions together that each use, even negotiate as a group for discounts from vendors. In
some cases, members can conduct business with each other or steer business to each other.
Although Hill de-emphasizes it, Edison, Firestone and Ford were as much about pragmatic alliance
as mastermind alliance.
7: “Extension” refers to the fact that you and I know many of the same people but we each know
different people. In mastermind alliance, by extension, your contacts are mine and mine are yours.
Members can make introductions or broker relationships for each other with their contacts.
8: As described earlier, there are choices to be made about the role and importance of the group
leader. If that leader is a sought after “guru” and access to him is relatively limited and parsed, then
intimate access to him in the mastermind group becomes a strong benefit. The more exclusive that
access, the bigger a selling benefit it is for the group.
9: When membership in the group confers status, in a closed community and/or to the member’s
marketplace, desire to be “in” rises as having it known one is “in” matters. This is trebly so, when
the group and/or the leader has a promotional platform from which the member’s status can
translate into commercial purpose and monetary gain.
14
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 20 --

10: With the cautions already given, the bundling of separately priced products and services as
well as special perks can act to effectively discount the mastermind group fee and improve the
value proposition and member’s ability to rationalize the fee.
11: Earlier, I described the portal approach. By this or other means, mastermind group members
can be given exclusive value not accessible by anyone outside the group. There is also a great
deal of income opportunity if this is pre-framed as a major benefit of being in the group, as that
removes “grumpiness” about the selling of things to them, and heightens their desire to buy and
use them – after all, they’re paying for access to them!
12: I am not personally a fan of incorporating this benefit. It adds hard cost. It can be a distraction
from delivery of real value. It can even have people in the group for the wrong reasons.
Nevertheless, a lot of mastermind groups have or are driven by this. Nanton and Dicks are a prime
example. This becomes about “place” – the meeting site, the exotic locale, the field trip, and about
“event” – in their case, taking the group to the Emmys, the Kentucky Derby, etc., and creating
events, like “Red Carpet Book Signings.”
THE MASTERMIND STORY
If you are going to sell a mastermind group, you are almost certainly going to use some version of
“the mastermind story.”
Most incorporate Napoleon Hill and Think And Grow Rich. I often utilize the chapter from Hill’s
Laws of Success (rather than or in addition to the material in TGR), to reprint and disseminate
and/or to draw from for a presentation or sales letter. These days, you have to back up with most
audiences and first tell the Carnegie/Hill origin story and create authority for Hill – there was a time
when, in business circles, everybody was familiar with it and show of hands tests of audiences
pulled 80% to 90% having read TGR. That is not the case today. But, cumbersome as it may be to
have to set up Hill before you can use him, I haven’t found a better means of giving importance to
the mastermind concept. Once this ground is laid, there are plenty of contemporary examples from
modern business to reinforce the concept, and you should find and use several that will be
meaningful to your particular audience. It is my opinion that the selling of the concept itself is
necessary, for new sales and for renewals. At the end of this, you’ll find excerpts from my letters
(renewal and new member) about Titanium that include the pitch for the concept itself, although
without relying on Hill as everyone receiving this is familiar with TGR.
Concepts that facilitate mastermind group sales are:
The 3 rd Mind – when two or more like-minded, capable people put their minds together for a
common purpose, an additional mind emerges more powerful than any of the individuals’ own
minds
Universal Intelligence – that the mastermind environment and activity opens the door to the
Universe’s Intelligence, always there
Super-Charged Subconscious - that the mastermind environment and activity “super stimulates”
the subconscious mind to draw on its rich storehouse
15
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 20 --

There will only be ONE Titanium Group in 2013.
I'll personally be conducting all three of its meetings.
It is THE only GKIC Mastermind Group that I am leading.
(The Platinum Info-Marketers Group is being managed by GKIC, and led by Dave Dee. Information about it
is available from GKIC.)
And there are new and improved member benefits.
A strict group size limit is 25, and I am actually looking for only 20, so I'm being
carefully selective.
In a moment, the details. First, though, I'd like to talk a bit about the mastermind
concept and process. If you insist you can skip to middle of the 3rct page. But if you just come
along with me, you might learn something! (Or, at minimum, pick up something you'll want to
use in presenting your own coaching opportunities to your clientele.)
IS IH-6R.6 MA~IC 	IN 	MASI6R.MIN):)7
nspontaneous Combustion,,- that's the benefit of a well-run, high-level
mastermind session that few can appreciate until they've experienced it. It 's what makes
it different and differently valuable than one-to-one consulting. By the melding of the
minds in the group, by the focus of all their intelligence and varied experience on a
single Member's business and subject for discussion he or she has chosen, insights occur
spontaneously that might never occur otherwise. One person's question or comment
sparks another's, that sparks another's, and ultimately brand new and unforeseen
solutions, strategies and opportunities are ignited. It actually IS an explosive situation, in a
very good way. I find that my own reservoir of knowledge and experience is plumbed
and probed and drawn from by the group in ways that it is never tapped by just a single
individual. I am "on the spot" the entire time , throughout the 2 days of each meeting.
Discussions go in directions the individual might never have planned. to arrive at better
destinations than he could have charted.
16
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 20 --

You can ask Bill and Steve Harrison about the price-breakthrough for their
National Publicity Summit or Dr. Chris Tom shack about the impetus for his entry into
franchising, leading to his current 360+ clinics across America - such things occurred
within one of my mastermind meetings. I can obviously give you a much longer list of
people who've exited one of my mastermind meetings with SPECIFIC AND
ACTIONABLE STRATEGY subsequently turned into millions of dollars.
There is also the radical or revolutionary breakthrough all can use.
exploding out of work on one member's business issues. This is how 'forced continuity'
came to the info-marketing industry. It's a norm now. It has made and makes fortunes. It
was brought to life in one of my mastermind meetings. The proper use of
"competitions" and grand-prizes to drive top-end coaching, which I began with Joe
Polish after some time spent with Bill Phillips (Body For Life) was refined and advanced
within one of my mastermind groups - then used very profitably by such info-marketing
luminaries as Rory Fatt of Restaurant Marketing Systems and Jay Geier of Scheduling.
Institute and many others. I could go on. Such things aren't predictable, yet they occur
often with me in my groups.
This is the "mastermind effect" that Napoleon Hill originally learned about from
Carnegie. Edison. Ford. Firestone and other inventors, industrialists and entrepreneurs
studied during Hill's 20 years' research, culminating in the books LAWS OF SUCCESS
and THINK AND GROW RICH. It is the creative force harnessed by copy and art teams
put together to work on campaigns, a sea change in the way advertising was created,
radical when first done during the 'Mad Men' Age. It has been a force behind the
greatest successes at Guthy-Renker, where Bill and Greg operate as a mastermind, then
organize different project teams ie. mastermind alliances for different purposes. I have
twice been engaged by them in the past year or so to lead such meetings.
I UNDERSTAND THIS PROCESS AS FEW DO- having led successful
mastermind groups for more than 20 years, and having been part of extraordinary
mastermind alliances for twice that.
For it to happen consistently, the explosive process must be harnessed, and the group
itself must be led by somebody with deep, rich and relevant experience, current relevant
work, and a capability for this particular leadership role.
No one who has ever been in a mastermind setting with me has failed to profit
directly from the "spontaneous combustion" -their gains emerging as often or more
often during discussions of others businesses as during discussions of their own. I have
been leading these kinds of "work groups" for more than 20 years, with info-marketers,
with "mixed breed" business owners, and in different niches. Titanium20 13 is THE
ONLY mastermind group for info-marketers that I'll be running, and I
will be leading all three meetings. And I do not blush when I say that you can
not find a better qualified and capable leader for a mastermind group for successful info-
marketers. I'll also say, loosely formed, less formal, leaderless groups are NEVER a tenth
as productive as mine. To think you can just pile wood in the room is fallacious.
Somebody who knows what they're doing needs to rub the sticks together.
(By the way, in my group, the rubbing together of the sticks demands real concentration and work from all. We WORK
TOGETHER for 9 to I 0 hours both days of every meeting. Don't come for a "spa 'n social experience" or all-night bar-hopping.)
17
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 18 of 20 --

There is a simple and obvious AND DRAMATIC multiplier effect. Twenty
smart and progressive people within a field sharing and exchanging information drawn
from their marketing tests, successes and flops, solving of problems, finding vendors, etc.
is a 19X against your own experience. This is a way to purchase speed.
There is a unique efficiency. Seven days of focus spaced during the year, out and
away from your regular day to day activities, away from interruptions and disruption,
work "on not in" the business in an idea-charged, experience-rich environment can
produce more clarity and actionable strategy than what might come from sixty days of
normal activity.
Finally. there is competition. In a group like this, no one wants to be the laggard.
Everybody wants to be THE biggest dog of the big dogs. That's good- it stimulates
everybody to do more, to implement more, to get smarter and to bring more. And in
2013, there will be (new) a Titanium Member of the Year, by vote of all
Titanium Members (and I get a vote) (you can't vote for yourself)- with the winner _by
group acclaim invited to the 20 14 GKIC Winners' Weekend or given comparable event
value if Winners Weekend replaced. And given "GKIC wide" recognition.
